1140 if method == "common": 1141 return round(value, precision) 1142 1143 func = getattr(math, method) 1144 return t.cast(float, func(value * (10**precision)) / (10**precision)) 1145 1146 1147 class _GroupTuple(t.NamedTuple): 1148 grouper: t.Any 1149 list: t.List 1150 1151 # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print 1152 # out the value during debugging. 1153 def __repr__(self) -> str: 1154 return tuple.__repr__(self) 1155 1156 def __str__(self) -> str: 1157 return tuple.__str__(self) 1158 1159 1160 @pass_environment 1161 def sync_do_groupby( 1162 environment: "Environment", 1163 value: "t.Iterable[V]", 1164 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1165 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1166 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1167 """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's 1168 :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for 1169 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171 unique value. 1172 1173 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174 can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1175 the ``city`` value of the group. 1176 1177 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1178 1179
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1180
  • {{ city }} 1181
      {% for user in items %} 1182
    • {{ user.name }} 1183 {% endfor %}
    1184
    • 1185 {% endfor %}
1186 1187 ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1188 can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1189 value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1190 1191 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1192 1193
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1194
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1195 {% endfor %}
1196 1197 You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1198 does not have the given attribute. 1199 1200 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1201 1202
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %} 1203
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • 1204 {% endfor %}
1205 1206 .. versionchanged:: 3.0 1207 Added the ``default`` parameter. 1208 1209 .. versionchanged:: 2.6 1210 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. 1211 """ 1212 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1213 return [ 1214 _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) 1215 for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) 1216 ] 1217 1218 1219 @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore 1220 async def do_groupby( 1221 environment: "Environment", 1222 value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", 1223 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1224 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1225 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1226 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1227 return [ 1228 _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) 1229 for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) 1230 ] 1231 1232 1233 @pass_environment 1234 def sync_do_sum( 1235 environment: "Environment", 1236 iterable: "t.Iterable[V]", 1237 attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None, 1238 start: V = 0, # type: ignore 1239 ) -> V:

37:__version__ = "3.1.0.dev0"
.. currentmodule:: jinja2

Version 3.1.0
-------------

Unreleased

-   Drop support for Python 3.6. :pr:`1534`
-   Remove previously deprecated code. :pr:`1544`

    -   ``WithExtension`` and ``AutoEscapeExtension`` are built-in now.
    -   ``contextfilter`` and ``contextfunction`` are replaced by
        ``pass_context``. ``evalcontextfilter`` and
        ``evalcontextfunction`` are replaced by ``pass_eval_context``.
        ``environmentfilter`` and ``environmentfunction`` are replaced
        by ``pass_environment``.
    -   ``Markup`` and ``escape`` should be imported from MarkupSafe.
    -   Compiled templates from very old Jinja versions may need to be
        recompiled.
    -   Legacy resolve mode for ``Context`` subclasses is no longer
        supported. Override ``resolve_or_missing`` instead of
        ``resolve``.
    -   ``unicode_urlencode`` is renamed to ``url_quote``.

-   Add support for native types in macros. :issue:`1510`
-   The ``{% trans %}`` tag can use ``pgettext`` and ``npgettext`` by
    passing a context string as the first token in the tag, like
    ``{% trans "title" %}``. :issue:`1430`
-   Update valid identifier characters from Python 3.6 to 3.7.
    :pr:`1571`

37:__version__ = "3.1.0.dev0"
.. currentmodule:: jinja2

Version 3.1.0
-------------

Unreleased

-   Drop support for Python 3.6. :pr:`1534`
-   Remove previously deprecated code. :pr:`1544`

    -   ``WithExtension`` and ``AutoEscapeExtension`` are built-in now.
    -   ``contextfilter`` and ``contextfunction`` are replaced by
        ``pass_context``. ``evalcontextfilter`` and
        ``evalcontextfunction`` are replaced by ``pass_eval_context``.
        ``environmentfilter`` and ``environmentfunction`` are replaced
        by ``pass_environment``.
    -   ``Markup`` and ``escape`` should be imported from MarkupSafe.
    -   Compiled templates from very old Jinja versions may need to be
        recompiled.
    -   Legacy resolve mode for ``Context`` subclasses is no longer
        supported. Override ``resolve_or_missing`` instead of
        ``resolve``.
    -   ``unicode_urlencode`` is renamed to ``url_quote``.

-   Add support for native types in macros. :issue:`1510`
-   The ``{% trans %}`` tag can use ``pgettext`` and ``npgettext`` by
    passing a context string as the first token in the tag, like
    ``{% trans "title" %}``. :issue:`1430`
-   Update valid identifier characters from Python 3.6 to 3.7.
    :pr:`1571`

lines: +    # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print
"""Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's
:func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for
+    You can pass ``with_counts=True`` to also get the number of items
+    in each group, without having to materialize and count the group
+    yourself with something like ``|list|length``. This yields
+        <ul>{% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
The attribute supports dot notation for nested access.
-        for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr)
+            for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr)
+    for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr):
-        for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr)
+            for key, v in groupby(sorted(values, key=expr), expr)
+    for key, v in groupby(sorted(values, key=expr), expr):

input.command: grep -n "def auto_to_list\|async def auto_to_list" -A 10 /workspace/repo/src/jinja2/asyncsupport.py 2>/dev/null; grep -rn "def auto_to_list" /workspace/repo/src/jinja2/

content.text: Good, matches original pattern. Now let's quickly test.

input.command:
python -c "
from jinja2 import Environment
env = Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items, count in users|groupby(\"city\", with_counts=True) %}{{ city }}:{{ count }} {% endfor %}')
users = [{'city':'NY','name':'a'}, {'city':'NY','name':'b'}, {'city':'LA','name':'c'}]
print(tmpl.render(users=users))

tmpl2 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|length }} {% endfor %}')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))
"

content.text: Both work. Let's test the async variant too and run the existing test suite for filters.

input.command:
python -c "
import asyncio
from jinja2 import Environment

env = Environment(enable_async=True)
tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items, count in users|groupby(\"city\", with_counts=True) %}{{ city }}:{{ count }} {% endfor %}')
users = [{'city':'NY','name':'a'}, {'city':'NY','name':'b'}, {'city':'LA','name':'c'}]
print(asyncio.run(tmpl.render_async(users=users)))
"
python -m pytest tests/test_filters.py -k group -q

content.text: All tests pass. Now let's run the full filter test suite quickly and generate the patch.

input.command: git diff -- src/jinja2/filters.py > patch.txt && cat patch.txt

diff --git a/src/jinja2/filters.py b/src/jinja2/filters.py index 80ea6504..90e6533e 100644 --- a/src/jinja2/filters.py +++ b/src/jinja2/filters.py @@ -1157,12 +1157,27 @@ class _GroupTuple(t.NamedTuple): return tuple.__str__(self) +class _GroupTupleWithCount(t.NamedTuple): + grouper: t.Any + list: t.List + count: int + + # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print + # out the value during debugging. + def __repr__(self) -> str: + return tuple.__repr__(self) + + def __str__(self) -> str: + return tuple.__str__(self) + + @pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, + with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for @@ -1203,6 +1218,20 @@ def sync_do_groupby(
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %} + You can pass ``with_counts=True`` to also get the number of items + in each group, without having to materialize and count the group + yourself with something like ``|list|length``. This yields + namedtuples of ``(grouper, list, count)`` instead. + + .. sourcecode:: html+jinja + +
      {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %} +
    • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
      • + {% endfor %}
    + + .. versionchanged:: 3.1 + Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. + .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. @@ -1210,10 +1239,20 @@ def sync_do_groupby( The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) - return [ - _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) - for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) - ] + + if not with_counts: + return [ + _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) + for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) + ] + + out = [] + + for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr): + values_list = list(values) + out.append(_GroupTupleWithCount(key, values_list, len(values_list))) + + return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore @@ -1222,12 +1261,24 @@ async def do_groupby( value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, + with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) - return [ - _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) - for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) - ] + values = await auto_to_list(value) + + if not with_counts: + return [ + _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(v)) + for key, v in groupby(sorted(values, key=expr), expr) + ] + + out = [] + + for key, v in groupby(sorted(values, key=expr), expr): + values_list = await auto_to_list(v) + out.append(_GroupTupleWithCount(key, values_list, len(values_list))) + + return out @pass_environment
